title: Production has no working email backend: allauth password reset and email verification cannot deliver (also dead-ends the merge gate)
claim: Neither base.py nor production.py sets EMAIL_BACKEND (only development.py:10 console and test.py:41 locmem do). Under config.settings.production this resolves to Django's default smtp backend at localhost:25 with DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL=webmaster@localhost. The live allauth /accounts/password/reset/ and email-verification flows send via smtp://localhost:25; a Fargate/ECS app container has no local MTA, so sends either 500 (ConnectionRefusedError) or silently never deliver, while allauth still renders a generic 'we sent you an email' success page (anti-enumeration). Local-account password reset is non-functional in production, and this dead-ends the one path (email verification) that could mark an EmailAddress verified and unlock the account-merge gate.
chain: production.py imports base.py; neither sets EMAIL_BACKEND -> Django default smtp localhost:25 -> /accounts/password/reset/ + email verification send_mail -> no MTA in container -> no delivery/ConnectionRefusedError; allauth renders generic success regardless

votes.evidence: CONFIG CLAIM REPRODUCED: grep of /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/config/settings/ shows EMAIL_BACKEND set only in development.py:10 (console) and test.py:41 (locmem). base.py has zero EMAIL_* or DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL settings (only ACCOUNT_EMAIL_* allauth flags, e.g. base.py:199 `ACCOUNT_EMAIL_VERIFICATION = "optional"`). production.py (read in full, 73 lines) contains only security headers + LOGGING — no email config. connectlabs.py does `from .production import *` and adds no email config. config/deploy.yml:27 sets `DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: config.settings.production` and defines no EMAIL env vars (and no code reads any). So production resolves to Django defaults: smtp.EmailBackend → localhost:25, DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL=webmaster@localhost. REACHABILITY: config/urls.py:83 `path("accounts/", include("allauth.urls"))` — /accounts/password/reset/, /accounts/signup/, /accounts/email/ are live routes, rendered with allauth's stock templates (no account/*.html overrides exist under templates/ or apps/). HOWEVER, the product's actual auth surface is apps/users/auth_urls.py → /api/auth/login/ + /api/auth/signup/; signup_view (apps/users/auth_views.py:137-174) does create_user + login() and sends NO email and creates NO EmailAddress row. frontend LoginForm.tsx has no forgot-password link and never references /accounts/ (grep for forgot/reset/accounts across frontend/src: zero hits); OAuth buttons use provider.login_url. grep for send_mail/EmailMessage across apps/ and config/: zero non-allauth senders. MECHANISM CORRECTION: with the default SMTP backend (fail_silently=False), an actual send attempt raises ConnectionRefusedError → 500 BEFORE the success page renders; the "renders generic success while silently not delivering" half of the claim doesn't occur on this config (and modern allauth's enumeration-prevention sends an "unknown account" email too, so even unknown addresses 500 rather than quietly succeed). MERGE-GATE: directionally real — local accounts get no EmailAddress at signup and the only delivery path for verification can't send — but social providers configured with VERIFIED_EMAIL=True (base.py:228/232/236) create verified EmailAddress rows without any email delivery, so verification is not exclusively email-dependent.
votes.notes: The configuration gap is 100% real and independently verified. Downgrades: (1) No surfaced user journey sends email — the SPA login/signup hit /api/auth/* which never touches the mail stack, and the frontend has no link to any /accounts/ allauth page; the broken flows are reachable only by manually navigating to allauth's unstyled default pages (or via the "Forgot your password?" link on allauth's own /accounts/login/ template, which the product never routes users to). So this is a latent 500 on unlinked routes plus a missing product feature (no password recovery exists in the UI at all, email backend or not), not a breakage of a flow users currently exercise. (2) Mechanism misstated: the failure mode on send is a hard 500, not silent success. (3) The merge-gate dead-end is real for local-only accounts, but project history (2026-06 incident) treats the unverified-email merge refusal as secure-by-design and it was resolved via operator merge; OAuth-originated accounts get verified EmailAddress rows without email delivery. Fix is still worthwhile: set EMAIL_BACKEND/DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL (e.g., SES/anymail) in production.py, or explicitly disable/unroute the allauth HTML flows if local password recovery is intentionally unsupported.

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced, including an empirical settings dump rather than trusting the finding's quotes.

1. EMAIL_BACKEND is set only in dev/test: `config/settings/development.py:10` (`EMAIL_BACKEND = "django.core.mail.backends.console.EmailBackend"`) and `config/settings/test.py:41` (locmem). Grep of all of `config/` confirms no EMAIL_BACKEND, EMAIL_HOST, DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL, anymail/SES/sendgrid, or any env() read of email vars anywhere in `base.py`, `production.py`, or `connectlabs.py` (which does `from .production import *` and adds nothing email-related).

2. Empirical reproduction: booted Django with `DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=config.settings.production` and printed resolved settings: `EMAIL_BACKEND: django.core.mail.backends.smtp.EmailBackend`, `EMAIL_HOST: localhost PORT: 25`, `DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL: webmaster@localhost`, `ACCOUNT_EMAIL_VERIFICATION: optional`. This is exactly the Django-default SMTP fallback the finding claims.

3. Production really runs this module: `config/deploy.yml:27`, `config/deploy-worker.yml:26`, `config/deploy-mcp.yml:27` all set `DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: config.settings.production`. Neither deploy.yml nor deploy-worker.yml contains any email/SMTP env vars (grep returned nothing) — and even if they did, no settings code reads them.

4. No local MTA: `Dockerfile:2` is `FROM python:3.12-slim`; no postfix/sendmail/exim/msmtp installed anywhere in the Dockerfiles. So smtp://localhost:25 has nothing listening -> ConnectionRefusedError on any send.

5. Reachability: `config/urls.py:83` includes `path("accounts/", include("allauth.urls"))`, and `allauth.account` is in INSTALLED_APPS (`base.py:64`) with `allauth.account.middleware.AccountMiddleware` (`base.py:93`), so `/accounts/password/reset/`, `/accounts/signup/`, and email-confirmation routes are live in production. A custom allauth layout template exists (`templates/allauth/layouts/base.html`), confirming these server-rendered pages are intentionally styled/served. With `ACCOUNT_EMAIL_VERIFICATION = "optional"` (`base.py:199`), local signup also triggers a confirmation-email send.

6. Merge-gate consequence: `base.py:209-214` (`SOCIALACCOUNT_EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION_AUTO_CONNECT`, `SOCIALACCOUNT_EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION`) rely on allauth's verified-email lookup; email confirmation is the only mechanism for a local account's EmailAddress to become verified, and that email cannot be delivered. (Consistent with the previously verified Connect-merge incident where an unverified EmailAddress caused the safe merge refusal.)

7. allauth is the only mail sender: grep for send_mail/EmailMessage/EmailMultiAlternatives across `apps/` and `config/` (excluding tests) found no other callers, so the blast radius is exactly the allauth flows.
votes.notes: Two minor mechanism refinements, neither changing the verdict: (1) The finding says allauth "still renders a generic 'we sent you an email' success page" — in the realistic container case (nothing listening on localhost:25) the SMTP ConnectionRefusedError propagates out of the POST handler, so the user gets a 500, not the success page; the silent-success branch would only occur if something accepted mail on localhost:25, which the python:3.12-slim image does not. The finding hedged with "either/or" so this is a clarification, not a refutation. (2) Discoverability is lower than "live UI path" might imply: the SPA LoginForm (`frontend/src/components/LoginForm/LoginForm.tsx`) has no "forgot password" or signup link — the broken flows are reachable only by navigating directly to /accounts/... URLs or via allauth-rendered pages. However, local-account signup itself (/accounts/signup/) would attempt a verification send under "optional" and likely 500, so the breakage is reachable today without any UI link. Fix is trivial: set EMAIL_BACKEND + DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL (e.g. anymail/SES) in production.py, or consciously set ACCOUNT_EMAIL_VERIFICATION="none" and disable local password reset.

votes.evidence: CONFIG DEFECT: independently reproduced, including an empirical settings boot. (1) grep of /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/config/settings/ shows EMAIL_BACKEND only in development.py:10 (console) and test.py:41 (locmem); base.py, production.py (read in full — only security headers + LOGGING) and connectlabs.py (`from .production import *`, adds login-redirect/iframe settings only) set no EMAIL_*/DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL. (2) Booted Django with DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=config.settings.production and printed resolved values: EMAIL_BACKEND=django.core.mail.backends.smtp.EmailBackend, EMAIL_HOST=localhost PORT=25, DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL=webmaster@localhost, ACCOUNT_EMAIL_VERIFICATION=optional. (3) Production really uses this module: config/deploy.yml:27, deploy-worker.yml:26, deploy-mcp.yml:27 all set `DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: config.settings.production`; no EMAIL/SMTP env vars in any deploy*.yml, and no settings code reads any. (4) Dockerfile:2 `FROM python:3.12-slim`, no postfix/sendmail/exim/msmtp anywhere — nothing listens on localhost:25, so any send raises ConnectionRefusedError. (5) allauth is the sole mail sender: grep for send_mail/EmailMessage/EmailMultiAlternatives across apps/, config/, mcp_server/ (excluding tests) returns zero hits. REACHABILITY: config/urls.py:83 `path("accounts/", include("allauth.urls"))` with allauth.account in INSTALLED_APPS (base.py:64) and AccountMiddleware (base.py:93) — /accounts/password/reset/ and /accounts/signup/ are live URLs in production, and templates/allauth/layouts/base.html exists (only a layout override; no account/*.html overrides). BUT no product surface routes users there: the SPA auth surface is /api/auth/login/ + /api/auth/signup/ (apps/users/auth_urls.py:28,36); signup_view (auth_views.py:137-174) does create_user + login() — sends no email and creates no EmailAddress row; frontend/src/components/LoginForm/LoginForm.tsx has no forgot-password or signup link; the only /accounts/ references in frontend/src are OAuth login URLs (OnboardingWizard.tsx:127 `/accounts/commcare/login/`, providers_view auth_views.py:255 `/accounts/{provider}/login/`), which send no email. LOGIN_URL is unset and the app's auth decorators (apps/users/decorators.py:18,36) return 401 JSON instead of redirecting, so nothing ever funnels a user to allauth's HTML login page (where the forgot-password link lives). MECHANISM: with the default SMTP backend and no listener on :25, the POST to /accounts/password/reset/ or /accounts/signup/ (verification=optional triggers a send at local signup) raises ConnectionRefusedError → 500 before any success page renders; the finding's "silently never deliver while rendering generic success" branch cannot occur on this config. MERGE GATE: apps/users/signals.py:104-111 refuses auto-merge without a verified EmailAddress; local accounts get no EmailAddress at all and the only verification path can't send — but base.py:225-238 sets VERIFIED_EMAIL=True for commcare_connect/commcare/ocs, so OAuth-originated accounts obtain verified EmailAddress rows with no email delivery; the gate is not exclusively email-dependent.
votes.notes: The configuration gap itself is 100% confirmed (empirically reproduced). Verdict is PARTIAL because two material aspects of the claim are misstated. (1) Status: BROKEN-NOW overstates it — no user journey the product exposes exercises an email send. The SPA login/signup hit /api/auth/* (no mail, no EmailAddress), the frontend never links to /accounts/password/reset/ or /accounts/signup/, LOGIN_URL is unset and API decorators 401 instead of redirecting. The broken flows are reachable only by manually typing /accounts/... URLs, so this is a latent 500 on live-but-unlinked routes plus an absent product feature (no password recovery exists in the UI at all). (2) Mechanism: the "allauth still renders a generic success page" half of the either/or is wrong for this config — ConnectionRefusedError propagates and the request 500s; there is no silent-success path without something accepting mail on localhost:25. (3) Merge-gate consequence is real but narrower than claimed: it dead-ends only local-password accounts; OAuth accounts via the three configured providers get verified EmailAddress rows without email, and project history treats the unverified-merge refusal as secure-by-design (resolved via operator merge). Agrees with prior verifier #1 (PARTIAL/LATENT); verifier #2's evidence is accurate but its BROKEN-NOW status conflates "route is live" with "flow is exercised". Fix remains worthwhile and trivial: set EMAIL_BACKEND + DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL in production.py (e.g. anymail/SES), or deliberately unroute/disable allauth's HTML account flows if local password recovery is unsupported.

title: allauth HTML auth surface bypasses the custom rate limiter, splits the brute-force budget, and shares per-process LocMemCache
claim: apps/users/rate_limiting.py (5/300s per email) is wired ONLY into login_view (auth_views.py:109) and signup_view (auth_views.py:150), not onto any /accounts/ view. allauth 65 does ship active defaults (login 30/m/ip, login_failed 10/m/ip + 5/300s/key, signup 20/m/ip, reset 20/m/ip + 5/m/key) so /accounts/login/ is not fully unthrottled, but: (1) the two surfaces use different cache keys (auth_attempts:<email> vs allauth/rl/...) and never share a budget — a brute force can be split across /api/auth/login/ and /accounts/login/ to double effective attempts; (2) both back onto LocMemCache (base.py:318-322, with the repo's own worker-warning comment at 324-325), so under N uvicorn workers the effective limit is ~Nx and every deploy resets all counters. Extends the known 'Chat rate limiting uses per-process LocMemCache' finding to the auth surface with the extra split-budget dimension.
chain: rate_limiting.check_rate_limit/record_attempt only at auth_views.py:109,150; allauth views use separate allauth RATE_LIMITS with different keys; both via django.core.cache -> base.py:318-322 LocMemCache per-process -> Nx under N workers + split budget across two surfaces

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced the full chain:

1. Custom limiter — apps/users/rate_limiting.py:11 `cache.get(f"auth_attempts:{username}", 0) >= 5` and :15 `key = f"auth_attempts:{username}"`, AUTH_MAX_ATTEMPTS=5 / AUTH_LOCKOUT_SECONDS=300. grep confirms the only non-test call sites are auth_views.py:109 (login_view) and :150 (signup_view). No /accounts/ view calls it.

2. allauth surface live — config/urls.py:83 `path("accounts/", include("allauth.urls"))`.

3. allauth 65.14.0 active defaults — read .venv/.../allauth/account/app_settings.py:261-300 RATE_LIMITS property: login_failed = `f"10/m/ip,{attempts_amount}/{attempts_timeout}s/key"` with unoverridden LOGIN_ATTEMPTS_LIMIT=5/TIMEOUT=300 → "10/m/ip,5/300s/key"; signup "20/m/ip"; login "30/m/ip"; reset_password "20/m/ip,5/m/key". No ACCOUNT_RATE_LIMITS or LOGIN_ATTEMPTS_* override in any settings module (grep returned nothing). So /accounts/login/ is NOT fully unthrottled — finding states this correctly.

4. Split budget — allauth get_cache_key (.venv/.../allauth/core/internal/ratelimit.py:93-114) keys as ("ip", ip) / ("user", pk) / (sha256(key),) tuples, completely disjoint from the custom `auth_attempts:<email>` string key. The two surfaces never share a counter; a brute-forcer alternating /api/auth/login/ and /accounts/login/ gets ~2x the effective per-email failure budget.

5. LocMemCache / Nx — both layers use django.core.cache → CACHES default = LocMemCache (config/settings/base.py:318-322) with the repo's own warning comment at base.py:324-325. No CACHES override in production.py/development.py/test.py. docker-compose.yml:45 launches `uvicorn ... --workers 4`, so the per-process cache multiplies all limits by ~4 and every deploy/restart wipes counters.

Minor note (does not weaken finding): custom limiter is email-keyed only with no IP throttle, while allauth adds IP limits — asymmetric but orthogonal to the claim. login_view uses email.strip() while signup_view uses .strip().lower(), a small case-normalization inconsistency, also not contradicting the finding.
votes.notes: All file:line references in the finding are accurate (auth_views.py:109/150, base.py:318/324). Severity LATENT/security is appropriate: it weakens brute-force defense (split budget + Nx + deploy-reset) but isn't actively causing data loss/breakage today, and allauth defaults provide partial coverage so the surface isn't wide open. Reachability confirmed: both /api/auth/login/ (SPA) and /accounts/login/ (allauth HTML) are wired to live routes; 4-worker deployment confirmed in docker-compose.

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced every link:

1. Custom limiter scope: /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/apps/users/rate_limiting.py:5-6 defines AUTH_MAX_ATTEMPTS=5, AUTH_LOCKOUT_SECONDS=300 keyed as `auth_attempts:{username}` (line 15). Repo-wide grep shows the only consumers are auth_views.py:109/114/117 (login_view) and auth_views.py:150 (signup_view). No /accounts/ view imports it.

2. Reachability of the allauth surface: config/urls.py:83 `path("accounts/", include("allauth.urls"))`. Runtime URL resolution confirms /accounts/login/, /accounts/signup/, /accounts/password/reset/ all resolve to allauth.account.views.view. base.py:195 `ACCOUNT_LOGIN_METHODS = {"email"}` plus AUTHENTICATION_BACKENDS containing ModelBackend (base.py:183-186) means /accounts/login/ is a live email+password login form today. No custom ACCOUNT_ADAPTER restricts it (only SOCIALACCOUNT_ADAPTER is set, base.py:217).

3. Allauth defaults active, so not unthrottled (finding correctly concedes this): no ACCOUNT_RATE_LIMITS anywhere in config/ or apps/. Runtime dump under project settings (django-allauth 65.14.0 per uv.lock) prints: login '30/m/ip', login_failed '10/m/ip,5/300s/key', signup '20/m/ip', reset_password '20/m/ip,5/m/key' — exactly as the finding states.

4. Split budget: allauth's limiter (allauth/core/internal/ratelimit.py:93-117) builds keys `allauth:rl:<action>:<sha256(key)>` — a disjoint namespace from `auth_attempts:<email>`. The two 5/300s-per-email budgets never interact, so an attacker alternating /api/auth/login/ and /accounts/login/ gets ~10 password guesses per email per 5-minute window instead of 5 (plus the independent per-IP limits, evadable by IP rotation).

5. Shared LocMemCache: allauth/core/internal/ratelimit.py:20 `from django.core.cache import cache` and rate_limiting.py:3 both hit the default cache; config/settings/base.py:318-322 configures only LocMemCache, with the repo's own warning comment at base.py:324-325 ("LocMemCache is per-process — rate limiting won't work across multiple workers"). development.py even logs "Using LocMemCache. Rate limiting and caching will not work across multiple workers." at startup. Under N workers the effective budget is ~N×, and every deploy/restart clears all counters.
votes.notes: Two minor corrections, neither affecting the verdict: (a) allauth cache keys are colon-joined (`allauth:rl:login_failed:<sha256>`), not slash-joined as written in the finding — cosmetic. (b) Bonus defect found while verifying, adjacent to the finding: signup_view (auth_views.py:150) calls check_rate_limit but never record_attempt, so the custom limiter on /api/auth/signup/ only ever blocks if prior *login* failures for that email tripped the counter — the signup-side throttle is effectively inert on its own. Status LATENT / impact security are appropriate: nothing is broken in single-worker dev, but in any multi-worker production deployment the brute-force budget is N× per process per surface, split across two unsynchronized keyspaces.

title: SOCIALACCOUNT_LOGIN_ON_GET=True + EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION_AUTO_CONNECT=True: login-CSRF and silent forced account-linking on GET
claim: base.py:204 SOCIALACCOUNT_LOGIN_ON_GET=True makes every /accounts/<provider>/login/ and ?process=connect an unauthenticated GET with no CSRF token (allauth docs flag this as login CSRF); the SPA relies on these GET anchor links (ConnectionsPage.tsx:235 ?process=connect, OnboardingWizard.tsx:127, LoginForm.tsx:113). Combined with SOCIALACCOUNT_EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION_AUTO_CONNECT=True (base.py:209), SOCIALACCOUNT_EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION=True (base.py:212), and every custom provider marked VERIFIED_EMAIL=True (base.py:225-238), a social login whose email matches an existing user is auto-connected with no confirmation. Exploit shapes: forced login (login CSRF) and forced account-linking. The OAuth state param protects the callback so it is not one-click takeover; residual risk is GET initiation + silent auto-connect, sharpest for the custom providers whose VERIFIED_EMAIL guarantee underlies the known OCS/merge findings.
chain: base.py:204 LOGIN_ON_GET=True -> /accounts/<provider>/login/ initiates OAuth on GET (no CSRF); allauth lookup applies EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION_AUTO_CONNECT (base.py:209) to silently link matching email to existing user; SPA uses ?process=connect GET links

votes.evidence:
I re-traced every link from settings to runtime behavior and the SPA, all quotes verified by my own reads.

1) Settings exactly as claimed in config/settings/base.py:
- Line 204: `SOCIALACCOUNT_LOGIN_ON_GET = True`
- Line 209: `SOCIALACCOUNT_EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION_AUTO_CONNECT = True`
- Line 212: `SOCIALACCOUNT_EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION = True`
- Lines 225-238: `SOCIALACCOUNT_PROVIDERS` with `commcare_connect`, `commcare`, `ocs` each `"VERIFIED_EMAIL": True` (228/232/236). All line numbers in the finding are accurate.

2) LOGIN_ON_GET=True genuinely bypasses CSRF on OAuth initiation (allauth 65.14.0). In `allauth/socialaccount/providers/base/views.py` BaseLoginView.dispatch (line 12-19): it calls `respond_to_login_on_get(request, provider)` then `provider.redirect_from_request(request)`. In `providers/base/utils.py:7-16`, `respond_to_login_on_get` only renders the CSRF-protected interstitial confirmation page when `(not LOGIN_ON_GET) and request.method == "GET"`. With LOGIN_ON_GET=True it returns None, so a GET to `/accounts/<provider>/login/` redirects straight to the provider — no interstitial, no CSRF token. This is exactly the allauth-documented login-CSRF surface. The `process` is read straight from `request.GET.get("process")`.

3) Silent auto-connect mechanism confirmed in `allauth/socialaccount/models.py`: `lookup()` (335-341) falls through to `_lookup_by_email()` (386-395) which selects existing local users by verified email (`e.verified`), and `_accept_login()` (397-405) then does `if app_settings.EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION_AUTO_CONNECT: self.connect(...)` with no user confirmation. VERIFIED_EMAIL=True is what makes the provider-supplied email count as verified so this gate fires.

4) Reachability is live, not theoretical:
- Route wired: config/urls.py:83 `path("accounts/", include("allauth.urls"))`.
- SPA GET anchor links confirmed: frontend/src/pages/ConnectionsPage/ConnectionsPage.tsx:235 `href={...${provider.login_url}?process=connect&next=...}`; frontend/src/components/OnboardingWizard/OnboardingWizard.tsx:127 `/accounts/commcare/login/?next=`; frontend/src/components/LoginForm/LoginForm.tsx:113 `${provider.login_url}?next=`. All are plain `<a href>` GET navigations.

The finding correctly hedges severity: it states the OAuth state param protects the callback (so this is NOT one-click takeover) and that residual risk is GET initiation (login-CSRF) plus silent auto-connect bounded by the provider's own email-verification guarantee. That characterization matches the code. The default `SOCIALACCOUNT_ALLOWED_EMAIL_DOMAINS` (base.py:247-252) only restricts `commcare` to dimagi.com; `ocs`, `commcare_connect`, google, github are unrestricted, so the adapter allowlist does not broadly mitigate. Status LATENT and impact security are appropriate.
votes.notes: One minor wording imprecision (not severity-changing): the finding calls `?process=connect` an "unauthenticated GET." The connect flow actually targets the currently authenticated session; the accurate part is that the GET initiation carries no CSRF token. The plain `/accounts/<provider>/login/` case (LoginForm/OnboardingWizard) is genuinely unauthenticated. This does not undermine the chain. The auto-connect-to-existing-user severity is gated by an attacker controlling a provider-asserted verified email matching a victim (sharpest for the Dimagi-operated VERIFIED_EMAIL providers), which the finding states explicitly. All quoted line numbers (base.py 204/209/212/225, ConnectionsPage.tsx:235, OnboardingWizard.tsx:127, LoginForm.tsx:113) verified accurate.

votes.evidence:
All config facts reproduced at the exact cited lines in config/settings/base.py: line 204 `SOCIALACCOUNT_LOGIN_ON_GET = True`; line 205 `SOCIALACCOUNT_AUTO_SIGNUP = True`; line 209 `SOCIALACCOUNT_EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION_AUTO_CONNECT = True`; line 212 `SOCIALACCOUNT_EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION = True`; line 214 `SOCIALACCOUNT_EMAIL_VERIFICATION = "none"`; lines 225-238 mark all three custom providers (commcare_connect, commcare, ocs) with `VERIFIED_EMAIL: True`. The in-code comment at base.py:210-212 itself states the intent: "Trust Dimagi-operated providers to have verified the email address on their end. Required so allauth's _lookup_by_email gate fires for these providers." — confirming the maintainers deliberately enabled the email-matching auto-link gate.

Installed library is django-allauth 65.14.0 (uv.lock), where LOGIN_ON_GET=True is the documented behavior that bypasses the intermediate CSRF-protected POST form and initiates the OAuth redirect directly on GET, and AUTO_CONNECT silently links a social login to an existing user whose (provider-verified) email matches.

Route reachability confirmed: config/urls.py:83 `path("accounts/", include("allauth.urls"))` mounts the allauth login/connect endpoints as live routes.

UI/SPA reachability confirmed in three live components:
- frontend/src/components/LoginForm/LoginForm.tsx:112-113 — `<a href={`${BASE_PATH}${provider.login_url}?next=...`}>` (GET anchor, login flow).
- frontend/src/pages/ConnectionsPage/ConnectionsPage.tsx:234-235 — `<a href={`${BASE_PATH}${provider.login_url}?process=connect&next=${BASE_PATH}/settings/connections`}>` (GET anchor, connect flow).
- frontend/src/components/OnboardingWizard/OnboardingWizard.tsx:127 — `<a href={`${BASE_PATH}/accounts/commcare/login/?next=${BASE_PATH}/`}>` (GET anchor).

The custom adapter (apps/users/adapters.py) does NOT add any CSRF or confirmation gate to initiation; its pre_social_login only enforces an email-domain allow-list post-callback, so it does not blunt the GET-initiation or auto-connect mechanics described.

The chain as stated is therefore real and reachable today via live routes + live UI.
votes.notes:
CONFIRMED on every code fact and on reachability; the finding is unusually well-calibrated and does NOT overstate (it self-rates LATENT and explicitly concedes "not one-click takeover" because the OAuth state param binds the callback to the victim's session). Two clarifications for accuracy, neither of which refutes the finding:

1. Minor imprecision: the finding calls `?process=connect` an "unauthenticated GET." The connect flow actually requires the user to already be authenticated to Scout (you connect a provider to your existing logged-in account); only the login flow is truly unauthenticated. The load-bearing point — that the GET *initiation* carries no CSRF token regardless of flow — holds for both.

2. Practical exploitability is genuinely bounded, as the finding itself states. Both "exploit shapes" are substantially mitigated: (a) login-CSRF cannot log a victim in as the attacker because the state param is bound to the victim's session and the attacker cannot inject their own authorization code; (b) forced account-linking via the victim's browser completes the provider auth with the victim's own provider session, not the attacker's. The sharpest residual risk is the AUTO_CONNECT + VERIFIED_EMAIL=True trust assumption: if any Dimagi-operated provider (notably OCS) ever returns an email it did not actually verify, an attacker who registers that email at the provider could take over the matching Scout account silently. That is a provider-trust contingency rather than a self-contained Scout code bug, which is exactly why LATENT (not BROKEN-NOW) is the correct status. Hardening recommendation consistent with allauth's own docs: set SOCIALACCOUNT_LOGIN_ON_GET=False (restore the CSRF-protected POST interstitial) and/or disable EMAIL_AUTHENTICATION_AUTO_CONNECT to force explicit user confirmation before linking by email.

title: One credential snapshot per materialization run with no mid-run/401 refresh: CommCare's 15-min OAuth TTL makes long CommCare OAuth syncs structurally impossible
claim: aresolve_credential runs once per tenant at worker-task start (proactive refresh only if <5 min to expiry, token_refresh.py:19); the token string is frozen into requests.Session headers for the whole run_pipeline invocation with no mid-run or 401-reactive refresh (materializer.py:96-127 reuses the same dict for discover+every source; commcare_base.py:47 bakes it into session headers at loader __init__). Verified upstream TTLs: CommCare HQ 15 min, OCS 10h, Connect 2 weeks. Any CommCare-OAuth materialization exceeding ~15 min hits 401 mid-load and fails (commcare_base.py:62-67 raises CommCareAuthError); because CommCare sources are non-resumable every retry restarts from zero with another <=15-min token, so large CommCare OAuth domains can NEVER complete. OCS latent (10h vs run), Connect low tail risk softened by cursor resume. The start-of-run variant of this exact bug already bit in deployment (commit d4dee2e, #112, AUTH_TOKEN_EXPIRED) and was fixed only at resolution time. Distinct from the known 'Connect-only retry hardening' finding — this is token-lifetime vs run-duration across all OAuth providers.
chain: tasks.py:264 aresolve_credential once per tenant -> credential_resolver.py:102 refresh only if <5min buffer -> static dict into run_pipeline on asyncio.to_thread -> commcare_base.py:47 token baked into session headers -> 15-min TTL passes mid-load -> commcare_base.py:62-67 CommCareAuthError on 401 -> run FAILED/PARTIAL; non-resumable -> retry restarts from zero -> can never complete

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced the full chain:

1. ONE CREDENTIAL SNAPSHOT PER TENANT: apps/workspaces/tasks.py:266 — `credential = await aresolve_credential(tm)` runs once per tenant inside the membership loop of `materialize_workspace`, then tasks.py:279-285 passes that frozen dict to `asyncio.to_thread(_run_pipeline_with_progress, tm, credential, pipeline_config, job_id)`. No re-resolution anywhere downstream.

2. REFRESH ONLY AT RESOLUTION TIME, <5-MIN BUFFER: apps/users/services/token_refresh.py:20 — `REFRESH_BUFFER = timedelta(minutes=5)`; token_refresh.py:43-51 `token_needs_refresh` returns True only within that buffer. apps/users/services/credential_resolver.py:100-113 `_aresolve_oauth_credential` refreshes only if `token_needs_refresh(token_obj.expires_at)` and on TokenRefreshError silently "uses existing token". The returned value is a plain string in a dict — no refresh handle travels with it.

3. NO MID-RUN OR 401-REACTIVE REFRESH: `grep -rn refresh mcp_server/` matches only a docstring in mcp_server/context.py. mcp_server/loaders/commcare_base.py:45-47 — `__init__` does `self._session.headers.update(build_auth_header(credential))` (token baked into requests.Session at loader construction); commcare_base.py:63-69 — `_get` raises `CommCareAuthError(f"CommCare auth failed for domain {self.domain}: HTTP {resp.status_code}")` on 401/403 with zero retry/refresh. No tenacity/backoff in any commcare_*.py loader (grep confirmed). The same frozen dict feeds discover (materializer.py:203) and every source (materializer.py:295 `_load_and_commit_source(..., credential, ...)` → materializer.py:761/764 `CommCareCaseLoader(domain=domain, credential=credential)` / `CommCareFormLoader(...)`).

4. COMMCARE NON-RESUMABLE: mcp_server/services/materializer.py:262 — `is_resumable_provider = pipeline.provider == "commcare_connect"`. CommCare sources always run with `resumable=False`, single commit at end (materializer.py:714-716), so a mid-load CommCareAuthError rolls back the in-flight source and any retry restarts that source from zero.

5. UPSTREAM TTL VERIFIED LIVE: fetched raw.githubusercontent.com/dimagi/commcare-hq/master/settings.py — line 889: `'ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE_SECONDS': 15 * 60,` with comment "expire the token every 15 minutes to match HIPAA constraints."

6. CONSEQUENCE: materializer.py:332-377 — the per-source exception handler marks the run PARTIAL/FAILED, skips remaining sources, and re-raises; tasks.py:307-309 records tenant failure. No path re-acquires a token.

7. REACHABILITY: live today. pipelines/commcare_sync.yml exists (provider: commcare, sources cases+forms); MCP agent tool `run_materialization` (mcp_server/server.py:521) and the API view (apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:185) both defer `materialize_workspace`. CommCare OAuth is a real supported credential: TenantConnection.OAUTH (apps/users/models.py:158) + resolver's commcare branch, and commit d4dee2e (#112) documents AUTH_TOKEN_EXPIRED hitting an actual deployment via the same frozen-token mechanism at resolution time.
votes.notes: Finding is accurate and if anything slightly understated: the "~15 min" budget is the BEST case. Resolution refreshes only when <5 min of lifetime remains, so a token with e.g. 6 minutes left is used as-is and the run can 401 after ~6 minutes of discover+load — the effective budget is anywhere from ~5 to 15 minutes. Two scoping qualifiers worth carrying: (a) only OAuth-credentialed CommCare connections are affected — API-key CommCare connections (build_auth_header "ApiKey" path, non-expiring) are a viable workaround; (b) "BROKEN-NOW" is conditional on a CommCare OAuth domain whose cases+forms sync exceeds the token budget — structurally guaranteed for large domains (1000-row pages, 120s read timeouts indicate these are expected), no-op for small ones. Also note `materialize_workspace` has no automatic procrastinate retry; "retry" means user/agent re-trigger, which still restarts CommCare sources from zero, so the "can never complete" conclusion holds. The OCS (10h TTL, latent) and Connect (2-week TTL, cursor-resumable) risk grading in the finding matches the code (materializer.py:262 resumable only for commcare_connect; ocs/connect TTLs not re-verified upstream but immaterial to the verdict).

votes.evidence:
Re-traced the full chain from live entry point to consequence, reading every file myself:

1. REACHABILITY (live): mcp_server/server.py:607 — the agent-facing run_materialization MCP tool calls `await materialize_workspace.defer_async(workspace_id=..., user_id=...)`. The task is wired and is the primary materialization path today.

2. ONE SNAPSHOT PER TENANT: apps/workspaces/tasks.py:265 — inside the per-tenant loop: `credential = await aresolve_credential(tm)`, then tasks.py:277-283 hands that single dict to `asyncio.to_thread(_run_pipeline_with_progress, tm, credential, pipeline_config, job_id)`. No re-resolution inside the run.

3. PROACTIVE-ONLY REFRESH: apps/users/services/credential_resolver.py:100-112 — `_aresolve_oauth_credential` refreshes only `if token_needs_refresh(token_obj.expires_at)`; apps/users/services/token_refresh.py:19 `REFRESH_BUFFER = timedelta(minutes=5)` and :49-51 (`returns False if expires_at is None`). So a token with 6 minutes left is used as-is. The module docstring (token_refresh.py:4) promises "reactively (after 401)" but no reactive caller exists in the materialization path.

4. TOKEN FROZEN IN SESSION: mcp_server/loaders/commcare_base.py:45-48 — `CommCareBaseLoader.__init__` does `self._session.headers.update(build_auth_header(credential))`; the same frozen dict is passed to discover and to `_load_and_commit_source(source.name, tenant_membership, credential, ...)` for every source (materializer.py ~295). `grep -rn refresh mcp_server/` finds zero token-refresh code — no mid-run or 401-reactive refresh anywhere in the pipeline.

5. 401 → HARD FAIL: commcare_base.py:63-70 — `_get` raises `CommCareAuthError` on 401/403; materializer.py catch-all marks the source `failed`, skips remaining sources, sets run FAILED/PARTIAL and re-raises.

6. NON-RESUMABLE: materializer.py — `is_resumable_provider = pipeline.provider == "commcare_connect"`; CommCare sources never get `start_cursor`/`cursor_callback`, and tasks.py's own comment confirms re-materialization "drops & recreates its raw_* tables". A retry restarts the whole tenant load with another at-most-15-minute token.

7. UPSTREAM TTLs VERIFIED: dimagi/commcare-hq settings.py: `ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE_SECONDS = 15 * 60` with comment "expire the token every 15 minutes to match HIPAA constraints" — so the cap is deliberate and won't be relaxed. dimagi/commcare-connect config/settings/base.py: `ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE_SECONDS: 1209600` (two weeks), matching the finding's Connect tail-risk framing. OCS 10h not independently verified (django-oauth-toolkit default is 36000s; plausible).

8. PRIOR ART: `git show d4dee2e` confirms the cited deployment incident — "MCP run_materialization never refreshed expired OAuth tokens before use, causing AUTH_TOKEN_EXPIRED errors" — fixed only with resolution-time proactive refresh, exactly as the finding states.

Conclusion: any CommCare-OAuth materialization whose wall time from token issuance exceeds ~15 min (less, if the token was already partially aged but outside the 5-min refresh buffer) hits 401 mid-load, fails, and a retry restarts from zero under the same ceiling — structurally unable to complete.
votes.notes: Minor corrections, none material: (1) cited line numbers drift slightly — aresolve_credential call is tasks.py:265, the 401 raise is commcare_base.py:63-70, refresh-buffer check is credential_resolver.py:102 via token_refresh.py:51. (2) The window can be WORSE than 15 min: refresh fires only when <5 min remain, so a run starting with a 6-minute-old token gets ~9 minutes; and if `expires_at` is None on the SocialToken, no refresh ever happens even at start. (3) OCS 10h TTL is the one upstream number I could not verify from source (DOT default makes it plausible); CommCare 15-min and Connect 2-week TTLs verified from dimagi/commcare-hq and dimagi/commcare-connect settings. (4) BROKEN-NOW is conditional on a real CommCare-OAuth tenant whose full load exceeds the token window — not provable from code alone, but the repo's own 120s read-timeout comment ("large CommCare domains may have slow API responses") and the d4dee2e production AUTH_TOKEN_EXPIRED incident show OAuth + long CommCare loads are both live in practice, so I do not downgrade to LATENT. API-key CommCare connections are unaffected, as the finding implies.

title: Refresh-failure falls back to a known-stale token and no 401 anywhere maps to re-authentication guidance; reactive-refresh path is dead code with false docstring
claim: Two interlocking gaps on the credential seam: (1) credential_resolver.py:107-111 catches TokenRefreshError and proceeds with the existing token it just determined is within 5 min of expiry or already expired ('using existing token'), rather than failing closed like the OCS team-mismatch guard 20 lines up — the run provisions a schema and burns the discover phase before dying at the first authenticated request. No branch in _summarize_error, ThreadJob.error_summary, or any resume prompt maps a 401 to 'reconnect your account' (the only credential guidance is the missing-credential case, tasks.py:1103-1108), so expired/revoked/wrong-permission are indistinguishable to agent and user. (2) token_refresh.py:3-5 docstring claims the service is called 'reactively (after 401)', but refresh_oauth_token has exactly two callers — credential_resolver.py:106 (proactive, task-start) and auth_views.py:240 (interactive providers poll); every loader 401 raises a provider AuthError that no handler maps to refresh-and-retry. The AUTH_TOKEN_EXPIRED envelope code (mcp_server/envelope.py:31, added with the #112 fix) has zero producers — only a test references the constant. Fixed-where-it-bit residue.
chain: credential_resolver.py:107-111 TokenRefreshError -> logger.warning + return stale token -> provision+discover with doomed token -> loader 401 -> AuthError -> error_summary verbatim, no reauth guidance (tasks.py:1085-1125); separately token_refresh.py:3-5 docstring claims reactive refresh but only proactive callers exist; envelope.py:31 AUTH_TOKEN_EXPIRED producer-less

votes.evidence: Re-traced independently. (1) credential_resolver.py:102-112: `if token_needs_refresh(token_obj.expires_at):` ... `except TokenRefreshError: logger.warning("Token refresh failed for provider %s, using existing token", provider)` then `return {"type": "oauth", "value": token_value}` — proceeds with the token it just determined is within REFRESH_BUFFER=5min of expiry (token_refresh.py:19,43-51). The OCS team-mismatch guard at credential_resolver.py:92 (`if not token_obj or _oauth_team_mismatch(...): return None`) fails closed by contrast. (2) Chain to wasted work: tasks.py:264 `credential = await aresolve_credential(tm)` -> tasks.py:277 `_run_pipeline_with_progress` -> materializer.py:183 `SchemaManager().provision(...)` and :186 `MaterializationRun.objects.create(...)` happen BEFORE :203 `_run_discover_phase` (first authenticated request). Loader 401/403 raises ConnectAuthError (connect_base.py:135-138 "Connect auth failed for opportunity {id}: HTTP {status}"), OCSAuthError (ocs_base.py:47), CommCareAuthError (commcare_base.py:64); grep over apps/ and mcp_server/ finds NO except-handler for any of these classes — they hit the generic `except Exception as e` at tasks.py:307-309 and become verbatim `error: str(e)`. (3) No reauth mapping in the job seam: _compose_failure_summary (tasks.py:64-122) emits verbatim source errors; resume prompts tasks.py:1085-1125 and error_summary branches tasks.py:1242-1257 mention credentials only for the no_runs case ("no credentials set up... check the workspace's connection", tasks.py:1103-1108, 1249-1253). Failing closed would have routed into exactly that message; the fallback bypasses it. (4) token_refresh.py:4 docstring says "Called proactively (before token expires) and reactively (after 401)" but grep shows exactly two callers of refresh_oauth_token: credential_resolver.py:106 and auth_views.py:240 (providers_view, also a proactive expiry check inside GET providers listing, not a 401 reaction). No refresh-and-retry exists anywhere. (5) AUTH_TOKEN_EXPIRED defined mcp_server/envelope.py:31; only other reference in the repo is tests/test_mcp_server.py:250-252 — zero producers, no error_response(AUTH_TOKEN_EXPIRED,...) call. Reachability: live — materialize_workspace deferred from mcp_server/server.py:607 (agent MCP tool) and apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:185 (API view); refresh_tenant_schema (tasks.py:160) shares the resolver.
votes.notes: Finding is substantially correct; minor inaccuracies: (a) no function named `_summarize_error` exists — the composer is `_compose_failure_summary` (tasks.py:64); (b) "burns the discover phase" — the run dies AT the start of discover (discover IS the first authenticated request), after provisioning the schema and creating the MaterializationRun row; (c) "expired/revoked/wrong-permission indistinguishable" is slightly overstated: the verbatim loader message carries the HTTP status ("HTTP 401" vs "HTTP 403"), so 401-vs-403 is technically visible in error_summary, though expired-vs-revoked is not and no actionable "reconnect" guidance exists in the chat/job path; (d) reconnect guidance does exist elsewhere in the product — ConnectionsPage.tsx:213,237 shows "Connection expired"+"Reconnect" driven by providers_view's proactive refresh attempt (auth_views.py:235-245) — but nothing links a materialization auth failure to that page, so the literal claim about the 401 path stands. Additional finding beyond the claim: credential_resolver.py:104 also silently falls through to the stale token when token_url is unknown or token_secret is absent, with no log at all. BROKEN-NOW is fair: the path triggers whenever a refresh token is expired/revoked (routine in production), and refresh_tenant_schema's fail-closed on credential=None (tasks.py:161-163) shows the codebase already treats unusable credentials as abort-worthy — the resolver just never reports the token as unusable.

votes.evidence:
Re-traced independently; every element of the claim reproduces.

(1) Fail-open on refresh failure — apps/users/services/credential_resolver.py:102-112: `if token_needs_refresh(token_obj.expires_at):` then `try: token_value = await refresh_oauth_token(...) except TokenRefreshError: logger.warning("Token refresh failed for provider %s, using existing token", provider)` and falls through to `return {"type": "oauth", "value": token_value}` — the near-expiry/expired token is returned as a usable credential. Contrast the fail-closed guard 10 lines up (line 92): `if not token_obj or _oauth_team_mismatch(membership, token_obj): return None`. token_needs_refresh (token_refresh.py:43-51) fires when `now + 5min >= expires_at`, so the fallback token is at best 5 minutes from death.

(2) Downstream chain — apps/workspaces/tasks.py:160 and :264 call `aresolve_credential`, then `run_pipeline` via asyncio.to_thread (tasks.py:173, 277). run_pipeline (mcp_server/services/materializer.py:96) provisions the schema and creates the MaterializationRun BEFORE any authenticated call (materializer.py:183-196), then DISCOVER at :203 (`_run_discover_phase(tenant_membership, credential, pipeline)`) is the first authenticated request — with a dead token it raises a provider AuthError (commcare_base.py:64, connect_base.py:135, ocs_base.py:47 all raise on 401/403). No `except *AuthError` handler exists anywhere in the main tree (grep for "except.*AuthError" returns zero hits outside tests/worktrees); the exception hits the generic handlers at materializer.py:404 / tasks.py:307 and is stringified verbatim. `_summarize_error` (materializer.py:663-673) returns `f"{exc.__class__.__name__}: {msg}"` — no 401→reconnect mapping. `_compose_failure_summary` (tasks.py:64-122) propagates that string verbatim into ThreadJob.error_summary (tasks.py:1255). The resume-prompt branches (tasks.py:1085-1125) contain credential guidance ONLY in the no_runs case at tasks.py:1103-1108 ("no credentials set up... suggest checking the workspace's connection") — exactly as the finding states; expired/revoked tokens fall into the generic failed/partial branches with the raw error string.

(3) Reactive-refresh claim is false — token_refresh.py:3-4 docstring: "Called proactively (before token expires) and reactively (after 401)." Grep shows exactly two non-test callers of `refresh_oauth_token`: credential_resolver.py:106 (proactive, task-start) and auth_views.py:240 (inside `providers_view`, a GET providers-status endpoint that refreshes near-expiry tokens on poll and marks them "expired" on failure). No 401-triggered refresh-and-retry exists anywhere.

(4) AUTH_TOKEN_EXPIRED is producer-less — mcp_server/envelope.py:31 defines the constant; repo-wide grep finds only the definition and tests/test_mcp_server.py:250-252, which merely imports it and asserts its value. `error_response` is never called with it.

Reachability: both consuming tasks are wired to live entry points today — `materialize_workspace.defer_async` at apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:185 and mcp_server/server.py:607 (agent tool), `refresh_tenant_schema.defer` at apps/workspaces/api/views.py:365. Token expiry + refresh failure (revoked/expired refresh token) is a routine production condition, so the opaque-error path is live, not hypothetical.
votes.notes: Two trivial mechanism nuances, neither changing the verdict: (a) DISCOVER is itself the first authenticated request (materializer.py:203 passes the credential), so a dead token kills the run AT discover — what gets "burned" is schema provisioning plus the MaterializationRun row (and in refresh_tenant_schema, the physical schema created at tasks.py:150 before credential resolution, later dropped). The finding's phrasing "burns the discover phase before dying at the first authenticated request" slightly overstates how far the run gets. (b) The stale-token fallback is not always doomed: token_needs_refresh fires 5 minutes before expiry, so a transient refresh failure with a still-valid token can succeed; the finding's own wording ("within 5 min of expiry or already expired") acknowledges this. The headline defects — fail-open contrasted with the adjacent fail-closed team-mismatch guard, zero 401→reauthenticate mapping anywhere in the error-summary/resume surface, false docstring, and the producer-less AUTH_TOKEN_EXPIRED constant — are all exactly as claimed.

title: Resume prompt else-branch tells the agent the materialization 'just completed ... using the now-loaded data' for fully FAILED and CANCELLED runs
claim: resume_thread_after_materialization branches on view_schema_failed / no_runs / partial explicitly (tasks.py:1075-1119), but status='failed' (all sources failed — exactly what token expiry before first commit or auth revocation produces) and 'cancelled' fall to the generic else branch (tasks.py:1120-1125): 'Materialization just completed (status=failed). Please continue with the user's original request using the now-loaded data.' For a total failure there is no loaded data; the instruction contradicts the embedded status and invites the agent to query empty/absent schemas — the documented precondition for the #190 panic-loop class. The 'partial' branch shows the intended honest pattern; 'failed' never got one. The resume task always fires via the finally block (tasks.py:356-360).
chain: tasks.py:1052 _aggregate_materialization_state returns status='failed' when any run FAILED -> tasks.py:1075-1119 if/elif covers view_schema_failed/no_runs/partial only -> tasks.py:1120-1125 else interpolates status into 'just completed ... using the now-loaded data' -> agent.ainvoke with that framing

votes.evidence:
Re-traced independently in /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/apps/workspaces/tasks.py:

1. Aggregation: `_aggregate_materialization_state` (line 928) returns `"cancelled"` if any run CANCELLED (lines 991-992, 1002-1003) and `"failed"` if any run FAILED (lines 994-995, 1004-1005). A total failure (e.g. auth/token failure before first commit) yields status="failed".

2. Branch coverage in `resume_thread_after_materialization` (line 1020): the if/elif chain handles only `view_schema_failed` (line 1085), `status == "no_runs"` (line 1096), and `status == "partial"` (line 1109). Note the view-schema check at line 1075 is gated on `status in ("completed", "partial")`, so it can never intercept "failed"/"cancelled". Both fall to the else at lines 1120-1125, whose exact text is:
   `f"{SYSTEM_RESUME_MARKER} Materialization just completed (status={status}). Please continue with the user's original request using the now-loaded data. Per-tenant: {summary}"`
   So for a fully FAILED or CANCELLED run the agent is told it "just completed" and to use "the now-loaded data" — there is no honest branch for "failed", unlike the carefully-worded "partial" branch (lines 1110-1118) which explicitly forbids querying failed sources.

3. Trigger always fires: `materialize_workspace`'s `finally` block (lines 356-360) calls `_defer_resume_for_job`, which defers `resume_thread_after_materialization` (line 393). Comment confirms it runs "ALWAYS ... even on early-return paths". CANCELLED ThreadJobs are deliberately claimable for resume (lines 1041-1043).

4. Reachability: live today. The MCP tool `run_materialization` (mcp_server/server.py:521) defers `materialize_workspace` (mcp_server/server.py:607) and is in the agent's allowed tool list (apps/agents/graph/base.py:71); the system prompt instructs the agent to call it (apps/agents/prompts/base_system.py:142). A second trigger exists via the periodic reconcile janitor (tasks.py:804). One carve-out: when the procrastinate job itself crashed (status "failed"/"aborted" at the queue level), reconcile flips the ThreadJob to FAILED without a resume (tasks.py:776-799) — but that only covers task-raise crashes, not the normal case where the task returns and MaterializationRun rows are FAILED.
votes.notes: Severity nuance (does not change the verdict): the else-branch body interpolates `status=failed` and the full per-tenant `summary`, which includes per-source `state: "failed"`, rows=0, and error strings — so the agent has the truthful detail available alongside the false framing. The codebase's own "partial" branch demonstrates the authors consider the raw summary insufficient without explicit do-not-query instructions, so relying on the agent to override "use the now-loaded data" from summary contents alone is exactly the gap. The fix is mechanical: add explicit `status == "failed"` and `status == "cancelled"` branches mirroring the honest "partial" wording. Also worth noting the "cancelled" case is arguably worse than the finding emphasizes — user-initiated Stop is a common path and gets the same "just completed ... now-loaded data" framing.

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced, all steps hold:

1. Total failure produces RunState.FAILED: mcp_server/services/materializer.py:355-371 — on a source exception with no committed rows, `any_committed` is False so `final_state = MaterializationRun.RunState.FAILED` (lines 359-363), the run row is saved, and the exception re-raises. Token expiry/auth failure on the first source before any commit hits exactly this.

2. The task survives and the resume always fires: apps/workspaces/tasks.py:307-309 — `materialize_workspace` catches the re-raised exception per tenant (`except Exception as e: ... tenant_results.append({... "success": False ...})`) and returns normally; tasks.py:356-360 finally block comment "ALWAYS defer the resume task" → `_defer_resume_for_job(job_id)` → `resume_thread_after_materialization.defer_async` at tasks.py:393 whenever a ThreadJob row exists (created by the live MCP run_materialization tool path; a second defer site exists in the reconcile janitor at tasks.py:804).

3. Aggregation: tasks.py:991-1005 — `if any_cancelled: status = "cancelled"; elif any_failed: status = "failed"`.

4. Branch coverage gap: tasks.py:1075 `if status in ("completed", "partial"):` gates the view-schema check, so "failed"/"cancelled" skip it; `elif status == "no_runs"` (1096) and `elif status == "partial"` (1109) don't match; both fall to the else at tasks.py:1120-1125: `f"{SYSTEM_RESUME_MARKER} Materialization just completed (status={status}). Please continue with the user's original request using the now-loaded data. Per-tenant: {summary}"`. For status="failed" with zero committed sources there is no loaded data; the instruction directly contradicts the interpolated status.

5. The message is fed to the agent: tasks.py:1144-1145 builds `HumanMessage(content=body)` and the task proceeds to `agent.ainvoke` (breadcrumb/log at 1128-1140 confirms this is the invoke path).

Reachability: live. The agent's MCP run_materialization tool creates the ThreadJob; the finally block fires unconditionally including the all-sources-failed case, since the procrastinate task itself succeeds (exceptions are caught per tenant at tasks.py:285-309).
votes.notes: Two accuracy nuances, neither changing the verdict: (a) the interpolated `Per-tenant: {summary}` does carry truthful per-source detail (state=failed + error strings, from _aggregate_materialization_state at tasks.py:964-982), so the agent gets contradictory rather than purely false information — the finding itself acknowledges this ("the instruction contradicts the embedded status"). (b) If the procrastinate task ITSELF crashes (job status failed/aborted before producing results), the reconcile path at tasks.py:776-799 flips the ThreadJob straight to FAILED with a synthetic failure message and never runs the misleading resume — so the defect manifests specifically when pipelines fail but the task completes, which is the normal/common failure mode (auth errors, ConnectExportError, any pipeline exception are all caught at tasks.py:289-309). The 'cancelled' half of the claim also holds: CANCELLED is deliberately claimable for resume (tasks.py:1041-1043) and lands in the same "now-loaded data" else branch, though cancelled runs may genuinely have some committed data, making it less acutely wrong than the fully-failed case. The 'partial' branch (tasks.py:1109-1118) is indeed the honest template that 'failed' lacks.

title: Connect Retry-After honored uncapped inside the single worker thread; '~14s worst case' comment false and the sleep is uncancellable
claim: connect_base.py sets respect_retry_after_header=True with 429/5xx in the forcelist; pinned urllib3 2.6.3's Retry.sleep sleeps the full server-supplied Retry-After uncapped (backoff_max bounds only the exponential path), up to 3 times per request, synchronously on the asyncio.to_thread pipeline thread. The comment at connect_base.py:31-32 promising '~14s worst case' is false whenever Retry-After is present. During the sleep the between-pages cancellation checkpoint (tasks.py:485-494) cannot fire, and on the single-worker deployment the platform's only background slot is held for the full sleep. Token-TTL interaction is minor for Connect's 2-week tokens but the worker-monopolization cost is real. Distinct from the known Connect retry-hardening finding (that one is about hardening absence; this is about an uncapped/uncancellable sleep blocking the sole worker).
chain: connect_base.py:61-69 Retry(respect_retry_after_header=True, 429 in forcelist) -> urllib3 2.6.3 Retry.sleep sleeps full header value uncapped, up to 3x/request -> runs inside _load_and_commit_source on the to_thread worker -> cancellation only checked between pages (tasks.py:485-494) -> sole worker slot held

votes.evidence:
Re-traced from pinned library to entry point.

(1) Retry config: mcp_server/loaders/connect_base.py:61-69 `_build_retry()` returns `Retry(total=3, backoff_factor=2.0, status_forcelist=[500,502,503,504,408,429], allowed_methods=["GET"], respect_retry_after_header=True, raise_on_status=False)`. Mounted on the session adapter at connect_base.py:128-130. Confirmed 429 in forcelist and respect_retry_after_header=True.

(2) CENTRAL CLAIM REFUTED — sleep is NOT uncapped. uv.lock pins urllib3==2.6.3. In .venv/.../urllib3/util/retry.py this version added `retry_after_max`: `DEFAULT_RETRY_AFTER_MAX: typing.Final[int] = 21600` (retry.py:206, comment line 204: "Setting to 6 hours"), with constructor default `retry_after_max: int = DEFAULT_RETRY_AFTER_MAX` (retry.py:231). `parse_retry_after` enforces it: "if seconds > self.retry_after_max: seconds = self.retry_after_max" (retry.py:336-337). `_build_retry()` does NOT pass retry_after_max, so the 6-hour cap is active. The finding states the sleep "sleeps the full server-supplied Retry-After uncapped (backoff_max bounds only the exponential path)." It is correct that backoff_max (DEFAULT 120, applied only in get_backoff_time retry.py:318) does not bound the Retry-After path, but it MISSES that retry_after_max does. So worst case per retry is 21600s, not the full server value; with total=3 the absolute ceiling is ~18h, but it is bounded, not "uncapped."

(3) "~14s worst case" comment misleading — CONFIRMED. connect_base.py:31-32 describes only the exponential 0/2/4/8 backoff path. retry.py:365-379 `sleep()`: if respect_retry_after_header and response, it calls sleep_for_retry and returns BEFORE _sleep_backoff — so when Retry-After is present, exponential backoff is skipped and the comment's "~14s" does not describe actual behavior.

(4) Synchronous + uncancellable inside to_thread — CONFIRMED. retries.sleep(response) is called at connectionpool.py:940 inside the synchronous .get(). Chain: apps/workspaces/tasks.py:277-282 `await asyncio.to_thread(_run_pipeline_with_progress, ...)` -> tasks.py:496 run_pipeline(...progress_updater=updater) -> mcp_server/services/materializer.py:295 _load_and_commit_source(... on_page=make_on_page(...)) -> loader._paginate_export_pages -> self._session.get() (connect_base.py:195). The cancellation checkpoint is the progress_updater raising MaterializationCancelled (tasks.py:489-494), invoked only via the on_page callback per page (materializer.py:148-178; module docstring lines 16-17: "Between pages it may raise MaterializationCancelled"). A sleep inside a single .get() cannot be interrupted by it. CONFIRMED.

(5) Reachability — CONFIRMED. Eight loaders (connect_users/visits/payments/completed_modules/invoices/assessments/completed_works + metadata) subclass ConnectBaseLoader and call _paginate_export_pages. Invoked from the live materialization pipeline task dispatched in apps/workspaces/tasks.py.

(6) Single-worker monopolization — PARTIAL. Procrastinate default concurrency is 1 (.venv/.../procrastinate/worker.py:28 `WORKER_CONCURRENCY = 1`), and the repo sets no WORKER_CONCURRENCY override, so a default worker does hold its only slot for the sleep. However, the committed docker-compose.yml (read in full) defines NO procrastinate worker service at all — the "single-worker deployment" is an inference about an unseen production setup, not verifiable in-repo.
votes.notes: Verdict PARTIAL: the defect is real and reachable (synchronous, between-pages-only-cancellable Retry-After sleep on the to_thread pipeline thread; the "~14s worst case" comment is genuinely false when Retry-After is present). But the finding's headline severity — that the sleep is "uncapped" / sleeps "the full server-supplied Retry-After" — is REFUTED by the pinned urllib3 2.6.3, which caps Retry-After at retry_after_max=21600s (6h) by default and is not overridden in _build_retry(). Realistic worst case is bounded (~6h/retry, ~18h ceiling across 3 retries), not unbounded. Two minor caveats: (a) "single-worker deployment" is an external assumption (default procrastinate concurrency IS 1, but no worker service exists in committed compose); (b) Connect's own server is unlikely to send multi-hour Retry-After values in practice, so the practical impact is far smaller than even the 6h cap — making this firmly LATENT/cost-perf, with the primary concrete bug being the inaccurate code comment plus the uncancellable-during-sleep window. Recommend correcting the finding to: (i) drop "uncapped"; (ii) note urllib3's 6h retry_after_max default; (iii) keep the comment-accuracy and cancellation-gap observations; (iv) optionally suggest setting retry_after_max explicitly to a small value (e.g. a few seconds) to make the bound intentional rather than relying on a 6h default.

title: Concurrent interactive token refresh (providers poll) can revoke the access token a long-running load is using mid-run
claim: providers_view (auth_views.py:235-243) refreshes near-expiry tokens whenever the settings/onboarding page polls /api/auth/providers/. django-oauth-toolkit's refresh grant revokes the prior access token server-side (standard DOT behavior, not verified against each provider's deployed version), so a user opening Settings during a materialization running on a near-expiry token would invalidate the token frozen in the run's session headers, killing the run minutes before its TTL. Narrow window (only inside the 5-minute refresh buffer) but applies to all three providers, including Connect's 2-week tokens in their final 5 minutes. Lowest-confidence item in the credential-lifetime cluster (hypothesis: depends on unverified upstream DOT revocation behavior).
chain: auth_views.py:235-243 providers_view refresh on poll -> DOT revokes old access token (upstream behavior, unverified) -> running loader session holds revoked token -> next page request 401 -> AuthError -> run fails

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain from real code.

(1) Trigger — providers_view IS live and IS authenticated-reachable. apps/users/auth_views.py:211 `@require_GET def providers_view`, wired at apps/users/auth_urls.py:30 `path("providers/", providers_view, name="providers")`. For an authenticated user it loads ALL the user's SocialTokens (auth_views.py:230-232) and for any within the 5-min buffer refreshes them: lines 235-243 `if token_needs_refresh(social_token.expires_at): ... async_to_sync(refresh_oauth_token)(social_token, token_url)`. No guard prevents this during an active run; grep for any lock/in-flight/active-run coordination found none.

(2) Refresh mutates the stored token. apps/users/services/token_refresh.py:84-90: `social_token.token = data["access_token"] ... await social_token.asave()`. The new access token replaces the old one server-side and locally. token_needs_refresh (line 43-51) fires when `now + REFRESH_BUFFER(5min) >= expires_at`.

(3) The running load freezes its token ONCE. apps/workspaces/tasks.py:160 and :264 call `credential = await aresolve_credential(membership/tm)` exactly once per tenant, then pass the resulting dict into the pipeline thread (tasks.py:173 `run_pipeline(membership, credential, ...)`, tasks.py:277 `asyncio.to_thread(_run_pipeline_with_progress, tm, credential, ...)`). aresolve_credential -> _aresolve_oauth_credential returns `{"type":"oauth","value": token_value}` (credential_resolver.py:112). Loaders bake that string into a persistent requests.Session header ONCE in __init__: mcp_server/loaders/connect_base.py:127 `self._session.headers.update({"Authorization": f"Bearer {credential['value']}"})`; commcare_base.py:47; ocs_base.py:43. The frozen session then drives a multi-request paginated loop: connect_base.py:186 `while url is not None:` issuing `self._session.get(...)` (line 195) for each page. A 401/403 mid-loop raises ConnectAuthError (connect_base.py:198-202), which propagates and fails the run via _drop_schema_and_fail (tasks.py:162/176). So if the in-flight access token is invalidated, the next page request 401s and kills the run — exactly the claimed consequence.

The mechanism inside Scout is fully reproduced and reachable. The ONLY unproven link is whether the OAuth *server* (CommCareHQ/Connect/OCS, all django-oauth-toolkit) revokes the prior access token on a refresh grant — Scout is the client and this is not in the codebase. The finding itself flags this as unverified/lowest-confidence, which is honest.
votes.notes:
PARTIAL, not CONFIRMED, for two reasons:

1. MECHANISM CORRECTION (factual): The finding repeatedly says the page "polls" / "polling" /api/auth/providers/. It does NOT poll. frontend/src/pages/ConnectionsPage/ConnectionsPage.tsx:77-104 fetches providers once on mount (useEffect) and once after a disconnect (line 167). frontend/src/components/LoginForm/LoginForm.tsx:26-30 also fetches once, but on the unauthenticated login page (request.user.is_authenticated is False there, so NO refresh path runs). So the real trigger is a single Settings/Connections page open by an authenticated user, not interval polling. This narrows frequency but does not eliminate the race — one fetch within the 5-min window is sufficient.

2. The load-bearing causal step (refresh grant revokes the old access token) is provider-server behavior outside this repo and was not verified against any deployed OAuth server. The finding acknowledges this. If the providers issue access tokens via JWT/self-contained validation or do NOT revoke-on-refresh, the old token stays valid until its own expiry and the race is benign. Given expires_in is honored (token_refresh.py:89) and the old token would expire within 5 min anyway, even in the revoke-on-refresh case the blast radius is just the final <5-min sliver of a token's life intersecting a run that hasn't itself already refreshed.

Status LATENT and impact correctness are appropriate. Severity is genuinely low: very narrow window, requires concurrent human action (opening Settings) during the last 5 min of a token on a still-running load, AND depends on unverified upstream revocation. The defect (no coordination/lock between the two independent refresh paths that both write social_token.token, and a frozen-at-start token in long loads) is real and accidental complexity, so REFUTED would be wrong. CONFIRMED would overstate certainty given the unverified upstream link and the incorrect "polling" mechanism.
